title: DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY 106, ‘76-82 (1984) CAMP-Stimulated Adenylate Cyclase Activation in Dictyostekm discoideum Is Inhibited by Agents Acting at the Cell Surface DONNAR.FONTANAANDPETERN. DEVREOTES”’
text:
DEVELOPMENTAL 
BIOLOGY 
106, ‘76-82 (1984) 
CAMP-Stimulated Adenylate Cyclase Activation in Dictyostekm 
discoideum Is Inhibited by Agents Acting at the Cell Surface 
DONNAR.FONTANAANDPETERN. 
DEVREOTES”’ 
Department 
of Biological 
Chemistry, 
Johns Hopkiw 
University 
School of Medicine, 
725 N. Wolfe Street, Baltimore, 
Maryland 
21205 
Received March 27, 1984; accepted in revised fm 
May 31, 1984 
The ability 
of Dictyostelium 
discoideum 
amoebae to synthesize 
and secrete CAMP in response to exogenous CAMP 
is called CAMP signaling. 
Concanavalin 
A is a potent, rapid, noncompetitive 
inhibitor 
of this response, with the rate 
of inhibition 
consistent 
with 
its rate of binding. 
The concanavalin 
A does not deplete 
cellular 
ATP, alter 
CAMP 
binding 
to its surface receptors, 
or affect basal adenylate 
cyclase activity, 
but blocks the CAMP-stimulated 
activation 
of adenylate 
cyclase. Therefore, 
concanavalin 
A appears 
to inhibit 
a step between 
the receptor 
and the adenylate 
cyclase which 
is necessary 
for the transduction 
of the CAMP signal. Wheat germ agglutinin, 
a polyclonal 
antibody 
against 
an 80-kDa 
glycoprotein, 
four 
monoclonal 
antibodies 
against 
the amoeba1 surface, 
and a chemical 
cross- 
linking 
agent which reacts with cell surface 
primary 
amines also inhibit 
signaling. 
To determine 
the importance 
of 
cross-linking 
in the inhibition, 
succinylated 
concanavalin 
A and the unlinked, 
reactive 
portion 
of the chemical 
cross- 
linker 
were tested and found to be relatively 
ineffective 
inhibitors. 
Thus it appears 
that ligands 
capable of cross- 
linking 
molecules on the external 
surface of D. discnideum 
amoebae inhibit 
CAMP signaling. 
It is proposed that these 
cross-linking 
agents prevent 
membrane 
or cytoskeletal 
rearrangement 
and that 
this 
rearrangement 
must occur 
before the adenylate 
cyclase is activated. 
c 1984 Academic 
Press, 1nc. 
INTRODUCTION 
Dictyostelium 
discoideum is an important model for 
studying cell-cell communication during development. 
In the life cycle of this organism, starved amoebae 
develop a system of chemical communication 
which 
consists of an adenylate cyclase, an external CAMP 
receptor, and phosphodiesterases (for a review see 
Devreotes, 1982). The binding of CAMP to its surface 
receptor transiently 
activates the adenylate cyclase 
(Roos and Gerish, 1976; Devreotes and Steck, 1979). 
The newly synthesized CAMP is secreted by the amoe- 
bae, while intracellular 
and extracellular 
phosphodies- 
terases act to return the CAMP concentration 
to its 
prestimulus level (Dinauer et ak, 1980a). This synthesis 
and secretion of CAMP in response to external CAMP 
is called signaling. With this communication 
system, 
a single amoeba which begins to secrete CAMP in a 
pulsatile manner can initiate CAMP waves which are 
relayed through 
the cell population 
(Tomchik 
and 
Devreotes, 1981). These waves plus an acquired che- 
motactic response to CAMP (Devreotes, 1982) result in 
the aggregation of lo5 to lo6 amoebae into a single 
multicellular 
unit. 
Attempts 
to activate the adenylate cyclase of D. 
discoideum 
in vitro 
by the addition 
of CAMP have 
failed. Therefore a search for inhibitors 
of this process 
1 To whom correspondence 
should be addressed. 
’ American 
Heart 
Association, 
Established 
Investigator. 
has been undertaken. A knowledge of how these inhib- 
itors function may give an insight into the steps which 
lead to activation of the adenylate cyclase in Dictyoste- 
hum. Brenner and Thorns (1984) recently reported that 
caffeine specifically inhibits adenylate cyclase activation 
in these amoebae and have speculated that caffeine 
acts by altering the intracellular 
calcium distribution. 
Here we report that concanavalin A (Con A), a plant 
lectin which binds to glucose or mannose residues 
(Goldstein, 19’76), also inhibits 
the CAMP-dependent 
stimulation 
of adenylate cyclase. Further studies sug- 
gest that Con A achieves this inhibition 
by cross- 
linking cell surface components. 
MATERIALS 
AND 
METHODS 
Cell growth, 
development, 
and perfusion 
assay. D. 
discoideum strain NC-4 was grown in association with 
Enterobacter 
aerogenes (Sussman, 1966) to a density of 
5 X lo* cells/100 X 15-mm Petri plate. The amoebae 
were harvested and washed free of bacteria with de- 
velopment buffer (DB, 10 mM Na-K PO1 buffer, pH 
6.9, 1 mM MgS04, 0.2 mM CaClJ. One milliliter 
of 
amoebae at 107/ml was shaken in DB in the presence 
of [3H]adenosine-labeled bacteria (Devreotes et al, 1979). 
After 3 hr the amoebae were allowed to settle on a 35 
X IO-mm Petri plate containing 
2% agar in DB and 
the fluid was removed. After developing for 3-4 hr 
more, the aggregating amoebae were resuspended in 2 
ml DB and 200 ~1 placed on a Millipore filter (type ss 
1300, Bedford, Mass.). These filters were part of a 4

77 
filter 
perfusion 
apparatus. 
When 
the effect 
of the 
monoclonal 
antibodies, 
3,3’-dithiobis(sulfosuccinimidyl- 
propionate) 
(DTSSP), 
and N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide 
(HOSu(S03)) 
was examined, 
the amoebae were shaken 
in the presence of the desired ligand for 30 min before 
being placed on the perfusion 
filter. 
The perfusion 
assay was used to quantitate 
CAMP 
signaling 
(Devreotes 
et ah, 1979; Dinauer 
et ak, 1980b). 
Briefly, the [3H]adenosine-labeled 
amoebae are perfused 
with 
buffer to remove the secreted phosphodiesterase 
and CAMP (Devreotes 
et aZ., 1979). The buffer 
can 
rapidly 
be replaced 
with 
other 
solutions 
containing 
inhibitors, 
CAMP, etc. The secreted solutions 
are col- 
lected, phosphodiesterase 
is inactivated 
(Dinauer 
et al., 
1980b), and the secreted CAMP is removed from other 
labeled, secreted compounds 
by sequential 
dowex and 
alumina 
chromatography 
(Salomon, 
1979). The cpm/ 
fraction 
is proportional 
to the CAMP secretion 
rate; 
this rate is directly 
proportional 
to the intracellular 
CAMP 
concentration 
and reflects 
activation 
of the 
adenylate 
cyclase (Dinauer 
et ak, 1980a). With 
two 4 
filter 
apparati, 
the relay response of control 
amoebae 
and those treated in 6 different 
manners can be deter- 
mined. 
CAMP binding 
assay. Amoebae were grown, developed 
(but not labeled with [3H]adenosine), 
and placed on the 
perfusion 
filters. Half of the filters were perfused with 
20 pg/ml 
Con A for 
6-10 min 
while 
the controls 
received DB. Before adding 
the rH]cAMP, 
the filters 
were removed and placed on waxed paper to prevent 
fluid flow. [3H]cAMP 
(10 nM) in DB containing 
10 mM 
dithiothreitol 
was added to the filters 
and allowed 
to 
set for 
30 sec. The [3H]cAMP 
solution 
was drawn 
through 
by placing 
the filter 
on 2 layers of Whatman 
3M paper. The amoebae were then washed twice with 
O”C, saturated 
ammonium 
sulfate 
(van Haastert 
and 
Kein, 1983), the filter 
placed in 0.4 ml of 1% sodium 
lauryl 
sulfate, and scintillation 
fluid added. Nonspecific 
binding, that rH]cAMP 
remaining 
which was not bound 
to a receptor, was measured by adding 100 pM unlabeled 
CAMP to the tritiated 
solution. 
Specific binding 
was 
defined 
as cpm (total) - cpm (nonspecific). 
The to- 
tal:nonspecific 
ratio was approximately 
3:l. 
Relative ATP levels. To determine 
relative 
levels of 
ATP, amoebae were developed 
in the presence of 100 
&i/ml 
of [32P]P04 and placed on the perfusion 
filters 
as already 
described. 
Some amoebae were pretreated 
for 6 min with 
20 pg/ml 
Con A, some with 
1 mM 
sodium azide, and others with buffer alone. The filters 
were then inverted 
into Falcon multiwell 
tissue culture 
plates 
(Cockeysville, 
Md.) containing 
300 ~1 ethanol 
(O’C). After 
5 min, the ethanol/cell 
extract 
was cen- 
trifuged 
for 
2 min 
in a microfuge 
and the pellet 
discarded. 
Aliquots 
of the supernatant 
were spotted 
on a PEI-cellulose 
thin 
layer 
chromatography 
plate 
and developed 
halfway 
in 0.5 M ammonium 
sulfate 
then placed in 0.7 M ammonium 
sulfate 
to complete 
development 
(Neuhard 
et ah, 1965). The plates were 
dried and autoradiographed. 
The 32P spots which 
co- 
migrated 
with 
an ATP 
standard 
were scraped 
into 
scintillation 
vials and counted. 
Adenylate cyclase assay. Adenylate 
cyclase activity 
was measured by placing 
filters 
containing 
developed 
amoebae into 300 ~1 of a reaction 
mix containing 
1 mM 
CAMP, 3 mM creatin 
phosphate, 
0.25 mM ATP, 12.5 
mM Tris pH 7.5, 1 mg/ml 
creatin 
phosphokinase, 
12 
mM CHAPS (3-[(3-cholamidopropyl)dimethylammonio]- 
1-propanesulfonate), 
1 mM MnC12, 1 mM MgCl,, 2 mM 
Na/K PO1 and trace amounts of [32P]ATP. The zwitter- 
ionic detergent, 
CHAPS, 
solubilizes 
the amoebae and 
allows 
the cyclase reaction 
to proceed (A. Theibert, 
personal 
communication). 
For activated 
adenylate 
cy- 
clase, the amoebae were placed in the reaction 
mix 1 
min after stimulation 
with 
1 pM CAMP. The reactions 
were stopped after 1 min by the addition 
of 2% sodium 
lauryl 
sulfate, 9 mM ATP, and 1.3 mM CAMP (Salomon, 
1979). The [32P]cAMP was then 
separated 
from 
the 
other 
radiolabeled 
compounds 
(Salomon, 
1979) and 
counted. 
Materials. 
Wheat 
germ 
agglutinin 
was purchased 
from 
Miles 
(Elkhart, 
Ind.) 
and Vector 
(Burlingame, 
Calif.) 
and succinylated 
Con A from 
Vector. 
DTSSP 
and HOSu(S03) 
were obtained 
from Pierce (Rockford, 
Ill), 
the PEI-cellulose 
plates from Brinkmann 
(West- 
bury, N. Y.), and radioisotopes 
from ICN (Irvine, 
Calif.). 
All other chemicals 
were purchased 
from Sigma (St. 
Louis, MO.). 
RESULTS 
A 6-min pretreatment 
with 
10 pg/ml 
concanavalin 
A strongly 
inhibits 
the CAMP signaling 
response of 
aggregation-competent 
D. discoideum (Fig. 1). The in- 
hibitory 
effect is prevented 
by preincubating 
the Con 
A with 
50 mM a-methylmannoside, 
a sugar to which 
Con A binds with high affinity, 
suggesting 
that Con A 
binding 
to an amoeba1 surface component(s) 
is neces- 
sary 
for 
inhibition. 
Treatment 
of Con A-inhibited 
amoebae with 
100 mM a-methylmannoside 
reverses 
the inhibition, 
presumably 
by displacing 
the Con A, 
indicating 
that the Con A treatment 
is not harmful 
to 
the amoebae. The dose-response 
curve 
reveals 
that 
Con A is a potent inhibitor 
with 50% inhibition 
occur- 
ring at 0.5 /*g/ml (Fig. 2). When the inhibition 
by Con 
A is partial, 
the subsequent relay response has kinetics 
similar 
to the control 
(Fig. lD, E is an example). 
To 
determine 
the time required 
for Con A to exert 
its 
effect, 100 ~1 of 100 pg/ml 
Con A was applied 
in a 3-

The above results 
showed that 
Con A is a potent, 
rapid, noncompetitive 
inhibitor 
of CAMP signaling 
in 
D. discoideum. 
Attempts 
were then made to determine 
the point of inhibition. 
Specific CAMP binding 
(10 nM 
CAMP) was measured 
and the untreated 
control 
cells 
bound 9.8 X lo3 molecules/cell 
while the Con A-treated 
cells (20 pg/ml, 
6 min) bound 9.5 X lo3 molecules/cell. 
Juliani 
and Klein (1977) have reported a slight increase 
in CAMP binding 
after a brief 
Con A treatment, 
but 
this was not observed. van Haastert 
and Kien (1983), 
by examining 
the effects of a series of CAMP analogs 
on CAMP binding, 
chemotaxis, 
and guanylate 
cyclase 
activation, 
observed that the receptors 
detected in the 
binding 
assay have the same specificity 
and affinity 
as 
those mediating 
chemotaxis 
and guanylate 
cyclase ac- 
tivation. 
Theibert 
and Palmisano 
have evidence, using 
these analogs, 
that 
the CAMP surface 
receptors 
re- 
sponsible 
for adenylate 
cyclase 
activation 
have the 
same specificity 
and affinity 
as those described by van 
Haastert 
and Kien 
(personal 
communication). 
These 
results 
imply 
one class of CAMP surface 
receptors. 
Because Con A does not inhibit 
CAMP-induced 
amoeba1 
shape changes (data not shown), 
does not block the 
CAMP-induced 
guanylate 
cyclase activation 
(Mato 
et 
ah, 1978), and there is no demonstrable 
effect of Con 
A on CAMP binding, 
the Con A inhibition 
of signaling 
is probably 
not the result of Con A preventing 
CAMP 
from binding 
to its surface receptor. 
In D. discoideum 
the addition 
of CAMP results 
in a 
rise in the intracellular 
level of CAMP prior 
to CAMP 
secretion 
(Dinauer 
et ah, 1980a), and Con A prevents 
this rise (Fig. 4). The absence of a rise in intracellular 
CAMP could be the result 
of (1) ATP depletion, 
ATP 
being the substrate 
for adenylate 
cyclase, (2) an acti- 
vation of an intracellular 
phosphodiesterase 
such that 
the CAMP is degraded shortly 
after 
its synthesis, 
or 
(3) a blockage 
at an intermediate 
step linking 
the 
CAMP surface receptor and the adenylate 
cyclase thus 
preventing 
activation 
of the enzyme. Con A appears 
not to affect ATP 
levels or basal adenylate 
cyclase

(Table 2). However 
when Con A is bound to 
the surface 
of an amoebae, binding 
of CAMP to its 
receptor does not result in activation 
of the adenylate 
cyclase (Table 2). 
Since no component 
of the transduction 
pathway 
between the CAMP surface receptor and the adenylate 
cyclase has been identified, 
the specificity 
of the lectin- 
induced 
inhibition 
of CAMP signaling 
was examined 
with 
the hope of identifying 
such a molecule. 
The 
tetravalent 
lectin, wheat germ agglutinin 
(WGA), 
has 
been shown to bind to D. discoideum 
(West et al., 1978; 
Burridge 
and Jordan, 1979) and WGA proved to be an

Since Con A and WGA are potent 
inhibitors 
of CAMP relay, either they both interact 
with a critical component(s) involved in signaling or 
possibly the mere presence of a ligand inhibits the D. 
discoideum CAMP signaling response. 
Antibodies were used to further 
examine whether 
binding to a specific surface component is necessary to 
exert inhibition. 
A polyclonal antibody against a pu- 
tative D. discoideum adhesion molecule, a glycoprotein 
with a molecular weight of 80,000 (gp SO), was obtained 
(gift of Dr. Bill Loomis). Western blot analysis against 
an extract of developed amoebae revealed that this 
antibody binds to at least 8 proteins (Murray 
et al., 
1981). When 50 pg/ml of the antibody was perfused 
past developed amoebae for 6 min, subsequent CAMP 
stimulation resulted in only 54% of the control response 
(Table 3). Four monoclonal antibodies 
against the 
surface of D. discoideum 
were also obtained (gift of 
Alan Smith and Dr. Steve Barclay). When amoebae 
are pretreated with these antibodies, the relay response 
is inhibited (Table 3). These monoclonal antibodies are 
not fully characterized, but it is known that there is 
no membrane protein 
which is bound by all four 
antibodies (S. Barclay, personal communication).

In solution

experiments. 
To examine the role of valency on the inhibition 
of 
CAMP relay, succinylated Con A (succinyl-Con A) was 
tested. Succinylation 
of Con A results in a dimeric 
molecule with unaltered affinity for cu-methylglucose 
and an unaltered ability to bind to cell surface receptors 
(Gunther et al, 1973). Because of the reduced valency, 
succinyl-Con A has a drastically 
reduced ability 
to 
carry out functions which involve aggregation of its 
receptors (for a review see Wang et al, 1976). Consistent 
with these observations, Weeks and Weeks (1975) have 
shown that succinyl-Con A, which does not cause cell 
agglutination, 
can block Con A-induced cell-cell agglu- 
tination in D. discoideum. Succinyl-Con A is relatively 
ineffective as an inhibitor 
of CAMP signaling (Fig. 5), 
with a pretreatment of 100 pg/ml for 6 min inhibiting 
the response by only 12%. 
Since the results with Con A, succinyl-Con A, WGA, 
and the antibodies suggest that the ability to inhibit 
the relay response appears to be related to valency 
and not to the identity 
of the bound molecules, the 
effect of the chemical cross-linking agent, DTSSP, was 
examined. This agent is divalent and interacts with 
primary 
amines on the cell surface (Starvos, 1982). 
DTSSP inhibited 
the CAMP relay response by 86% at 
10 mM (Fig. 6). If the amoebae are treated with the 
unlinked 
reactive 
portions 
of 
this 
molecule, 
(HOSu(SO,)), only 9% inhibition 
is observed at twice 
this concentration. These results suggest that the mode 
of inhibition 
is cross-linking surface components.

concentration

device. The amoebae were then 
perfused 
with 
buffer 
for 30 min before 1 nM CAMP was applied 
for 
6 min. The concentration 
of HOSu(SOa) being compared with DTSSP 
is twice 
that of DTSSP because each molecule 
of DTSSP contains 
two molecules of HOSu(SOJ. 
The points represent 
at least duplicate 
determinations. 
DISCUSSION 
Several investigators 
have shown that D. discoideum 
development 
is affected by the addition 
of the lectin 
concanavalin 
A. Low concentrations 
of Con A (less 
than 
50 pg/ml 
with 
cells 
at 2 X 107/ml) 
stimulate 
development 
(Darmon 
and Klein, 1976) whereas higher 
concentrations 
delay or block it (Gillette 
and Filosa, 
1973; Weeks and Weeks, 1975; Darmon 
and Klein, 
1976). High concentrations 
of Con A have been shown 
to inhibit 
the CAMP chemotactic 
response (Gillette 
and 
Filosa, 
1973), to cause a premature 
rise in cellular 
phosphodiesterase 
(Gillette 
and Filosa, 
1973; Darmon 
and Klein, 
1976), and to increase 
the level 
of an 
extracellular 
phosphodiesterase 
inhibitor 
(Darmon 
and 
Klein, 1976). Because the amoebae bind and internalize 
Con A (Condeelis, 
1979) or extrude 
their bound Con A 
receptors into the medium (Gillette 
et al, 1974; Hellio 
and Ryter, 
1980) and because these responses or the 
assays for them are slow, the concentrations 
of Con A 
necessary 
to exert 
the previously 
reported 
effects is 
uncertain. 
The only rapid 
responses to CAMP which 
have been measured 
in the presence of Con A, the 
CAMP-induced 
rise in cGMP and the CAMP-induced 
decrease in light scattering, 
were unaffected 
by Con A 
at 10 pg/ml 
(treated 
in solution) 
and at 100 pg/ml 
the 
rise in cGMP was enhanced whereas the light scattering 
response was inhibited 
(Mato et ah, 1978). 
Con A, WGA, 
DTSSP, 
anti-gp80, 
and 4 different 
Inhibition 
of Cyclme 
Activation 
81 
monoclonal 
antibodies 
inhibit 
the D. discoideum 
CAMP 
signaling 
response. Although 
Con A and WGA recognize 
surface carbohydrates, 
they differ 
in specificity 
with 
Con A preferring 
mannose and glucose residues 
and 
WGA 
preferring 
(N-acetylglucosamine)z 
(Lis 
and 
Sharon, 1977). On D. discoideum 
most of the receptors 
for WGA are distinct 
from those for Con A (Molday 
et ah, 1976; West et aZ., 1978; Hellio 
and Ryter, 
1980). 
Since all 5 antibodies 
tested were also inhibitory, 
it is 
unlikely 
that the binding 
to a single surface component 
blocks the signaling 
response. 
The results 
obtained 
with 
Con A versus succinyl- 
Con A, and DTSSP versus HOSu(SOJ 
indicate 
that 
cross-linking 
surface components 
causes inhibition. 
If 
the level of inhibition 
is controlled 
by the degree of 
cross-linking 
of surface components, 
it is possible that 
the monoclonal 
antibodies 
did not have enough binding 
sites to affect full 
inhibition. 
This would 
explain 
the 
apparent saturation 
of the monoclonal 
antibody-induced 
inhibition 
at less than 100%. Con A and WGA would 
have sufficient 
binding 
sites to induce near-complete 
inhibition 
(Weeks, 1975; Darmon 
and Klein, 1976; West 
and McMahon, 
1977). DTSSP, which 
reacts with 
pri- 
mary amines on the cell surface (Starvos, 1982), should 
also be capable of extensive 
cross-linking. 
Although 
Con A and antibody-induced 
cross-linking 
often leads 
to capping, 
the rate 
at which 
the Con A inhibits 
signaling 
is too fast for Con A-induced 
capping 
to be 
involved 
(Gillette 
et al., 1974; Condeelis, 
1979; Hellio 
and Ryter, 
1980). This conclusion 
is supported 
by the 
WGA-induced 
inhibition; 
WGA does not cause capping 
in D. discoideum 
(Molday 
et al., 1976; Hellio 
and Ryter, 
1980). 
Altering 
the surface, 
apparently 
by cross-linking, 
blocks the how of information 
from the occupied CAMP 
surface 
receptor 
to the adenylate 
cyclase, thus pre- 
venting 
activation 
of this enzyme without 
affecting 
its 
basal activity. 
It has been shown that during 
CAMP 
signaling 
the number 
of vesicles and empty vacuoles 
within 
the amoebae increases 
and the cell surface 
becomes highly 
irregular 
whereas before the response 
it was smooth 
(Maeda and Gerisch, 
1977). The time 
course of the rise in vesicles is consistent 
with 
these 
structures 
being involved 
in adenylate 
cyclase activa- 
tion. Studies with Con A, WGA, and cationized 
ferritin 
have shown that as long as these ligands 
occupy the 
majority 
of the D. discoideum 
amoeba1 surface phago- 
cytosis 
cannot 
occur, 
with 
particle 
adsorption 
and 
membrane 
fusion 
blocked (Hellio 
and Ryter, 
1980). If 
membrane 
shuttling, 
e.g., endocytosis 
or exocytosis, 
is 
a vital 
part of signaling, 
as suggested by the electron 
microscopy 
results, 
it is reasonable 
that its blockage 
would eliminate 
the response. Alternatively 
the cross- 
linking 
agents may be affecting 
the cytoskeleton 
or
